title: VP: Interview video with Prof. Walter C. Willett
text:
[Music]
Uh yes. Uh well as you say we have for a
long time been investigating how diet
affects our health and uh we have
identified the components that we see to
be a healthy diet and the interesting
thing is I I think this is a healthy
dietary pattern for almost everyone uh
even at their even if they're at higher
risk. Uh what we usually see is that the
benefit of a healthy diet is uh
important for almost everyone. Uh and
it's more important for some people and
maybe less important for other people,
but pretty much everybody would benefit
from a healthier diet. The main
difference is uh the degree to which it
is is beneficial. uh the uh if someone
is very lean and active and other
doesn't smoke and otherwise is very
healthy their risk of disease is usually
it will be less less risk of heart
attacks or diabetes would be relatively
low. So they will usually benefit less
by healthy diet but still they benefit
somewhat that uh and we've looked at uh
for weight gain for example at the type
of carbohydrate in the diet and that
seems to be quite important uh to have
more whole grains less refined grains.
Uh but uh what we when we look by the
genetic risk, it's um it uh the the
benefit is greater for people who have a
high genetic risk, but it's still good
for people with a low genetic risk, even
though not it's not as important, but
it's pretty interesting that this is
more we we really haven't seen a good
example where the diet would go in an
opposite direction. The health effects
would be in an opposite direction for
different people.
[Music]
Yes. Um actually uh there's a lot of uh
work ongoing in this area and I don't
think we have all the answers yet but uh
and some people will uh maybe will
benefit from some individualized dietary
advice. uh but uh for example some
people are more sensitive to sodium or
salt in the diet and they would benefit
greater by reducing the salt to quite a
low level but almost everybody develop
has some benefit for uh reducing the
sodium intake in their diet. So uh I
think it's and that's an important point
because it's the main public health
tools that we have are things like uh
policy changes uh economic subsidies or
taxes and cultural change and that
doesn't work very well for individuals.
Those are really cultural changes that
help people. That's the most powerful
way to move a population and individuals
to a healthier diet is to shift the
whole cultural change. For example, uh
it used to be here in the US that uh
almost all men smoked tobacco. Uh but
we've had a big cultural change that I I
I can go a week and I never see anyone
smoke tobacco. It's it's just not
something that would be socially
acceptable in most uh situations in most
social groups or in in individuals. Uh
but when I was a student, people smoked
in the classroom even. It's just not
imaginable. So, uh the most effective
changes are actually not individual
changes. They're they're cultural
changes where we move the the whole
population toward a healthier diet or or
lifestyle in general.
Now the the work we're doing on
precision medicine uh I think can be
helpful. We'll probably learn some
things from that about how the diet is
working. But I don't think it will if
you really want to have a healthy
population. I I don't think it will be
precision medicine that is going to make
that happen. It it really has to be the
broad impacts of a cultural change and a
shift toward a healthier diet with some
people maybe needing some extra
attention like sodium reduction or
something like that. But in general, the
most important part is to to make it
easier for everybody to have a healthy
[Music]
diet. Yes. Well, yes, you're right. The
topic of ultrarocessed foods is getting
a lot of attention, but I think it's
actually a distraction. Uh that uh
clearly we all agree that there are a
lot of unhealthy foods uh and especially
uh sugar sweetened beverage, coke for
example, uh uh and processed meats. Uh
so if you create a score that has
processed meats and coke or soda,
sugarsw sweetened beverages in that
score, of course that's going to look
bad. even if there are healthy things in
the in the part of the score. So, but
what we see is that the classification
of ultrarocessed foods uh includes some
foods that are actually quite healthy
like whole grain foods u and a lot of
foods that are not ultrarocessed are not
healthy like uh too much white rice is
not considered an ultrarocessed food but
that's not a healthy food. uh it would
be better to be having whole grain rice
and uh too much red meat is not
ultrarocessed but that's not good for us
either. So I think this ultrarocessed
idea is really a distraction. We should
focus more on the things that we know
are important like whether a food is
whole grain or refined grain very
important or whether it has too much
sodium or too much sugar uh or unhealthy
fats. Those are the things that are
really important. And of course, not too
much red meat and plenty of fruits and
vegetables. That's what really makes a
healthy diet. And this idea, this
ultrarocessed idea, there's too many
false negatives and too many false
positives.
[Music]
Uh yes uh as when we started our work uh
that's a very important point when we
started our work back in
1970 1980 uh the main emphasis was to
reduce total fat in the diet. But when
we started looking at our data, the
long-term follow-up in our populations,
we didn't see that total fat was related
to anything really. Uh it was really the
type of fat, the quality of the fat is
very important. And the first thing that
we saw that was really bad was trans
fat, which is caused by the partial
hydrogenation of plant oils. Uh
fortunately in China, there was never
there was never too much never very much
uh partially hydrogenated fat in the
food system. And I think that's because
the traditional fats of China were
mostly liquid fats anyway. And so people
were using the plant oils without
partially hydrogenating them, which is
good. Uh but uh in many countries around
the world, most of the plant oils, the
plant fats were being partially
hydrogenated. That creates trans fats
and they're by far the worst type of
fat. Uh we also see that there's a
little increase in risk of
cardiovascular disease and diabetes with
saturated fat. Uh but then very strong
benefits of unsaturated fat particularly
polyunsaturated fat that's both
essential and uh uh and also reduces
blood cholesterol. uh has lots of
antioxidants in it that uh I actually
don't remember seeing data on the
effects on u endothelial function but
almost for sure that will be beneficial
as well because there are
anti-inflammatory
uh effects of plant oils. There's a
misconception that omega-6 fatty acids
are pro-inflammatory. That's simply
wrong. That was a hypothesis. But all
the studies show no increase in
inflammation. Much many studies show
increase. Many studies show decrease in
inflammation with
omega-6
polyunsaturates. So we need both omega-6
and omega-3. There's been controversy
about that, but we clearly need them
both. They both have good health
effects. So that would be things like
soybean oil, peanut oil, uh rape seed
oil. Those are uh very healthy fats as
long as we don't partially hydrogenate
them. So when we replace saturated fat
with that with those healthy plant oils
then we really get a double win where we
reduce the saturated fat we increase the
polyunsaturated fat. I know in much of
the world, including China, palm oil is
a major source of fat uh because it's
it's probably the least expensive. And
I've uh I brought samples from Beijing,
from Shanghai, from McDonald's, and
we've analyzed them in our laboratory,
and it's all it was all palol that we
saw. And that's not such a good type of
fat. It's uh much better if it's uh more
uh polyunsaturated like again peanut
oil, rape seed oil or soybean oil. Those
are very good uh uh types of types of
fat to use and they have both omega6 and
omega-3 fatty acids in it. So type of
fat is really important.
[Music]
Yes. Well, that's this is really a
worldwide global problem. You're
absolutely right that cardiovascular
disease is the number one cause of death
in almost all countries right now. And
uh it's increasing often now because of
diabetes being a when we uh look across
the world the changes that are happening
are happening very quickly. Usually the
first thing we see is an increase in
diabetes then excuse me first thing we
see is an increase in obesity then we
see diabetes then we see cardiovascular
disease then kidney disease uh and then
dementia. uh these these things follow
in that that order starting with obesity
it with poor diet and less physical
activity being the major causes of that
but then the complications occur over
decades later. So uh in many parts of
the world we're just in the early phase
of this epidemic with obesity and
diabetes but there's what much worse
things to come in the future. So diet is
really important in preventing the
diabetes, the weight gain uh in the
beginning and the overall dietary
pattern that we see again many uh uh the
overall dietary pattern with a generous
amount of fruits and vegetables and
whole grains not refined grains. Uh
small amount of red meat if we like that
maybe one serving of dairy a day. Uh
that's actually a uh that was what we
found in our analysis. It turns out
that's the recommendation for China. But
not more than one serving a day of dairy
a day. That's enough. Uh and uh of
course limiting sodium and especially
limiting sugar sweetened beverages.
That's really important. So if we look
around the world at the changes that are
happening, I would first uh try to
reduce sugar sweetened beverages. That's
number one problem actually. Uh and then
uh shifting more from refined grains to
whole grains. uh again plenty of fruits
and vegetables and focusing more on
animal sources of protein rather than
plant focusing more on plant sources of
protein rather than animal sources of
protein. Of course, each culture uh each
area will have different vegetables,
different fruits, different flavors. So
there's a you know thousands of
different ways of putting that together.
Maybe ma said let many flowers bloom.
That applies to diets.
[Music]
Well, I think u nutrition is really I
think a wonderful area to work in
because it has uh for science for
interest in understanding biology it is
really so interesting so complicated uh
and we're still learning a lot about how
diet works uh and ways to help people
have better diets, but it's also has
direct benefits for health of people and
whole populations at the same time. So
it's really quite nice to work in an
area where you can be at the cutting
edge of science whether it's genetics or
metabolomics or proteomics or uh public
health and it has at the same time has
direct benefits for people uh to help
them live happier lives and better
lives.
[Music]
